coredata.dc:title: Isoniazid (INH) a tuberculosis drug detection using turn-off fluorescent [Ce (o-Van)3 (H2O)3] probe in real samples

coredata.dc:description: Isoniazid (INH) is an essential antibiotic for the treatment of tuberculosis (TB). The accurate determination of its concentration in the human body is essential and must be monitored carefully because of the risk of severe side effects from prolonged use or overdosing. In this work, we synthesized a turn-off fluorescent sensor based on the coordination complex [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] by a simple precipitation method using cerium (III) nitrate hexahydrate and ortho-vanillin (o-Van) in a 1:3 stoichiometric ratio. The Ce3+ ion of the complex is coordinated by three bidentate o-Van ligands and three water molecules. Structural, optical and morphological characterizations were done using HRMS, FT-IR, UV–Vis spectroscopy, XPS, XRD, SEM, and TGA was used to assess thermal stability. The sensor has been proposed to work from the mechanism where the > C=O group of the amide functionality of INH replaces a coordinated water molecule in the complex, leading to fluorescence quenching. With an optimized condition, the fluorescence emission spectra showed the linear response within the concentration ranges 14.47 μM − 147.966 μM with an excellent correlation coefficient value of R2 = 0.994. Thus, the proposed sensor had a lower limit of detection (LOD) of 1.63 μM for the INH drug. the as-synthesized [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] probe provides excellent results for the selective detection of INH in pharmaceutical tablets, human urine, and blood samples of healthy people.

head.ce:title: Isoniazid (INH) a tuberculosis drug detection using turn-off fluorescent [Ce (o-Van)3 (H2O)3] probe in real samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology (Banaras Hindu University), Varanasi, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Technology
Indian Institute of Technology (Banaras Hindu University)

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors have contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for the sensing of INH using a fluorescence probe [Ce (o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex.

Isoniazid (INH) is an essential antibiotic for the treatment of tuberculosis (TB). The accurate determination of its concentration in the human body is essential and must be monitored carefully because of the risk of severe side effects from prolonged use or overdosing. In this work, we synthesized a turn-off fluorescent sensor based on the coordination complex [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] by a simple precipitation method using cerium (III) nitrate hexahydrate and ortho-vanillin (o-Van) in a 1:3 stoichiometric ratio. The Ce3+ ion of the complex is coordinated by three bidentate o-Van ligands and three water molecules. Structural, optical and morphological characterizations were done using HRMS, FT-IR, UV–Vis spectroscopy, XPS, XRD, SEM, and TGA was used to assess thermal stability. The sensor has been proposed to work from the mechanism where the > C=O group of the amide functionality of INH replaces a coordinated water molecule in the complex, leading to fluorescence quenching. With an optimized condition, the fluorescence emission spectra showed the linear response within the concentration ranges 14.47 μM − 147.966 μM with an excellent correlation coefficient value of R2 = 0.994. Thus, the proposed sensor had a lower limit of detection (LOD) of 1.63 μM for the INH drug. the as-synthesized [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] probe provides excellent results for the selective detection of INH in pharmaceutical tablets, human urine, and blood samples of healthy people.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Isoniazid, also known as Pyridine-4-carbohydrazide or isonicotinic acid hydrazide (INH), is a widely used first-line anti-tuberculosis drug. First and second-line drugs are the general classifications of anti-tuberculosis medicines [1]. The first-line medicines used to treat drug-sensitive tuberculosis include INH, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol. Second-line medicines, typically used for drug-resistant TB, consist of aminosalicylic acid, ethionamide, thioamides, protionamide, rifapentine, rifabutin, capreomycin, and various fluoroquinolones [1,2].

INH is particularly valued for its high capability, low toxicity, and good tolerance, making it a key component in treating tuberculosis, either as a monotherapy or in combination with other drugs, most commonly rifampicin [3–5]. It is effective against both pulmonary and extrapulmonary forms of the disease, including meningitis and genitourinary infections [4–6]. However, long-term use or excessive dosages of INH can lead to adverse effects such as thrombocytopenia, hepatotoxicity, neurotoxicity, allergic skin reactions, and endocrine disorders [3,7]. Tuberculosis caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis [8,9], is a chronic infectious disease and the second most common cause of infectious disease-related deaths globally [10,11]. Clinically/ therapeutically, the optimal daily dose of INH recommended for adults is approximately 4–6 mg/kg [12,13]. Therefore, developing a simple, sensitive, and selective probe to detect INH is crucial for both pharmacological research and clinical treatment.

Various analytical methods have been used for the detection of INH, including UV–visible spectrophotometry method [6,14,15], Fluorometric method [7,9], Polarography technique [16,17], Electrochemical technique [18], High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) technique [19,20], chemiluminescence process [21,22], and Capillary electrophoresis [23,24]. While each method offers specific advantages, they also exhibit certain limitations. For instance, chemiluminescence, spectrophotometry, and capillary electrophoresis methods often lack sufficient sensitivity [25]. Although electrochemical techniques are commonly used for INH detection, they suffer from poor repeatability and challenges in electrode adjustment, limiting their applicability in real samples [26,27]. HPLC, particularly when coupled with mass spectrometry (MS) techniques such as HPLC-MS/MS, is widely employed due to its high sensitivity [28], but it’s a costly and time-consuming technique.

Among these methods, fluorescence-based techniques are particularly advantageous for detecting such analytes, offering a balance of sensitivity, rapid response, and comfort of usage. In this present work, we synthesized a novel fluorescence sensor, [Ce(o-Van)3 (H2O)3], for the detection of INH. This sensor is highly significant and desirable due to its rapid response, simple synthesis, low cost, good sensitivity, reliability, and excellent selectivity.

Now, to synthesize our fluorescent coordination complex, we selected cerium (Ce) due to its cost-effectiveness among the lanthanides, making it a cost-effective choice. With a weight percentage of 0.0046 %, cerium is one of the most commonly occurring rare-earth elements in the lanthanide series and can be found in the Earth's crust [29,30]. Cerium (Ce3+) ions are mostly used in several analytical methods because of their two most stable oxidation states (Ce3+ and Ce4+), which mostly support some geometries having coordination numbers (CN) from 6 to 10 [29,31,32]. The electronic configuration of cerium [Xe] 4f1 5d1 6 s2, therefore, the Ce3+ is responsible for the good fluorescent, mainly in the form of Ce (III)-based coordination complexes, over Ce4+ due to the empty available 4f orbital [29,31,32] Besides, Certain organic ligands and their coordination mechanisms have a significant impact on the fluorescent characteristics of Ce (III)-based coordination complexes. Moreover, Cerium ions are sensitive to the appropriate probe that is shielded from the nonradiative deactivation process [29,31].

Similarly, 2-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde, also known as the 3-methoxysalicylaldehyde or o-Van, can bind the rare earth metal ions and transition metal ions through the coordination interaction of phenolic and methoxy oxygen [33–36]. In this work, a novel fluorescent [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] coordination complex (probe) has been synthesized, in which the o-vanillin acts as a bidentate ligand and is strongly bound with the Ce3+ central metal ion. HR-MS initially confirms the synthesis of the fluorescent metal–ligand coordination complex, and Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. Then, it is further characterized by ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and SEM for its chemical, structural, and morphological analysis. The thermal stability of materials is investigated with the help of a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA), which is shown in Fig. S6. This fluorescent probe is then successfully applied to detect isoniazid (INH) in pharmaceutically available isoniazid tablets, urine, and blood samples of healthy persons.

Ortho-vanillin [o-Van] (assay ≥ 98 %) was purchased from Avra, and cerium (III) nitrate hexahydrate [Ce (NO3)36H2O] (assay ≥ 99 %) was purchased from HIMEDA. L-lysine (C6H14N2O2) (assay 98–103 %) and D-(+)-glucose anhydrous (C6H12O6) (assay ≥ 99.5 %) were also purchased from HIMEDA, India. Bovine serum albumin (95 %), Isoniazid [C6H7N3O] (assay ≥ 99 %), Magnesium nitrate hexahydrate (MgN2O6·6H2O) (assay ≥ 98 %), and Creatine hydrate [C4H9N3O2·H2O] (assay ≥ 99 %) from Sigma-Aldrich. Sodium chloride (NaCl) (assay ≥ 99 %), Glycine [H2NCH2COOH] (assay ≥ 99.5 %), Calcium chloride dihydrate [CaCl2·2H2O] (assay ≥ 97 %), and Sodium hydroxide pellets (NaOH) (assay ≥ 97 %) were obtained from Merck, India. L-ascorbic acid (C6H18O6) (99.7 %) and Potassium sulphate [K2SO4] (99.5 %) were obtained from SDFCL, India. Uric acid [C5H4N4O3] (assay ≥ 99 %) and L-cysteine (C3H7NO2S) (assay ≥ 99 %) were obtained from SRL, India. The chemicals listed above were utilized without any additional purification.

Various characterization tools are utilized to confirm the material's structural and morphological characteristics. SCIEX X500R Q-TOF high-resolution mass spectrometer (HR-MS) is used to characterize the synthesized materials from the 100–950 m/z range. Thermo Scientific Nicolet 6700- FTIR spectrometer was used to perform the analysis of Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) from a spectral range of 550–4000 cm−1. Ultraviolet–visible (UV) spectra were recorded from 200-800 nm wavelength to characterize the liquid complex using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600) SHIMADZU. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were recorded on EVO- scanning electron microscope MA 15/18 instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed on Thermo Fischer Scientific ESCALAB Xi+. The crystal structure of the as-synthesized [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex was investigated using a Rigaku mini flex 600 X-ray diffractometer. F-4600 FL Spectrophotometers were used to characterize photoluminescence (PL) spectra using an aqueous solution of the Cerium-ortho-Vanillin [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex and Alpha 300, WITECH has been used to perform the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra.

The [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] nine coordination complex is synthesized as per the previously reported co-precipitation method [33–35,37], represented in a schematic diagram (Scheme 1 ). Briefly, in this method, the 0.15 M (456.5 mg) of 2-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde (o-Van) was dissolved in 10 ml ethanol, and in another flask, 0.05 M (434.6 mg) of Cerium (III) nitrate hexahydrate [Ce (NO3)36H2O] were also solubilized in 10 ml double distilled water. The solution of [Ce (NO3)36H2O] is regularly dropwise added to the alcoholic solution of ortho-vanillin at 60 °C temperature under continuous stirring. When 0.92 M NaOH solution was added to the reaction mixture, a yellow precipitate started to form, and the pH was adjusted to 8. The mixture was maintained at approximately 60 °C with continuous stirring for 3 h. The reaction was then allowed to continue for 10 h with the same stirring conditions after the temperature was raised to 95 °C. After that, the precipitate was filtered, and it was selected to allow the reaction mixture tocool to room temperature. The synthesized [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] coordination complex was thoroughly washed with deionized water and vacuum-dried overnight at 80 °C temperature. High-resolution mass spectrometry (HR-MS) was used to initially confirm the synthesis of the complex, which is described in the electronic supplementary information (ESI) S3.

After confirming the synthesis through HRMS, the as-synthesized coordination complex was initially investigated through FTIR, UV–vis spectroscopy, and XRD. TGA is used to investigate the thermal stability of materials (Fig. S6). The organic functionalities of both the ortho-vanillin ligand and the as-synthesized [Ce(o-Van)3 (H2O)3] complex, which were recorded by FT-IR spectroscopy, are exhibited in Fig. 1
(a). The spectra of ortho-vanillin and the [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex are relatively similar, except for a few differences in the mode of vibrational frequencies. The IR peaks are observed at 3200–2800 cm−1 in ortho-vanillin due to intramolecular hydrogen bonding between C=O and OH [38,39]; the disappearance of these peaks from the spectrum of the complex indicates the participation of phenolic oxygen in complexation after deprotonation [40,41]. The stretching frequencies in ligands observed at 2835 and 1456 cm−1 are due to the aromatic (phenolic) C-O bond, and this frequency is shifted towards the lower wavenumber at 2763 and 1434 cm−1 in the complex, which indicates that the oxygen of the aromatic C-O is also coordinated with the complex [40,42]. The FT-IR stretching frequencies of free ligand (2-hydroxy-3-methoxy benzaldehyde) and [Ce (o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex appear at approximately 1641 cm−1 due to the C=O bond of the carbonyl group (–CHO), indicating the aldehyde group is not coordinated with the Ce3+ ion in the complex [35]. The characteristic peaks in the metal complex were detected at 2934 cm−1, which may be assigned due to the C–H vibrations, which are usually not significantly different from that of the ortho-vanillin [43]. The complex contains water molecules that coordinate with Ce3+ ions, resulting in a broad peak at 3438 cm−1 caused by –OH vibrations [29,35,37,40,44,45].

After that, the formation of [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex and its interaction with INH was investigated by UV–visible spectroscopy in the range of 200–800 nm, which is exposed in Fig. 1 (b). It is clear that the appearance of an absorption peak in Cerium nitrate at 251 nm is due to the 4f − 5d transition [29,46]. However, the spectrum of the partially yellow-colored solution of Ce (III) complexes displays the broadband transition at 371 nm and two sharp peaks at 264 and 217 nm due to π-π* or n-π* transition in the phenyl ring [35,47] and 4f − 5d transition [48–50]. In the evaluation of the free metal ion (Ce3+) exhibiting a peak at 251 nm, the shifting of.

The absorption peaks agree with a crystal-field effect on the 5d orbitals of the Ce3+ due to the coordination of ligand to metal (Ce-O) [29,46]. The absorption spectra of INH appear at 260 nm, which is also plotted in Fig. 1 (b) [51]. After the addition of INH, the shifting of absorption peaks (371, 264, and 217) of the complex toward the longer wavelength (386, 275, and 233 nm) evidenced the INH interaction with the complex.

Next, Fig. 1 (c) presents the X-ray diffraction patterns for both the [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex and the ligand O-Van. The data indicate distinct differences between the patterns of O-Van and the [Ce (o- Van)3(H2O)3] complex. The peaks observed in the complex are relatively broader compared to those of the ligand, likely due to the formation of a distorted lower-symmetry crystalline structure, which is common for coordination complexes [29]. The morphology of this coordination complex material was analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fig. 1 (d) depicts the SEM image of the prepared coordination complex at a 1 μm scale, revealing particles with a typical rod-shaped morphology. The XPS and Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) were used to analyze the elemental composition of [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3]. The SEM images at different scales are provided in Fig. S1 of electronic supplementary material (ESI). Fig. 1 (e) presents the EDS spectrum for the complex, representing the presence of elements Ce, O, and C. The EDS analysis shows a uniform distribution of Ce, O, and C on the surface of the developed complex.

The elemental analysis, bonding state of all existing elements, and oxidation number of central metal (Ce3+) ions in the [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex was recorded with the help of a high-resolution XPS spectrophotometer as shown in Fig. 2
. [29,52–54]. First, the possible elements present in the sample surface were detected by the survey scan (Fig. 2 (a)), and the corresponding peaks were identified. The survey spectrum of complex displays the existence of Cerium, Oxygen, and Carbon elements with an approximate atomic percentage of Ce3d5 (7.77 %), O1s (49.23 %), and C1s (43.44 %) in the material. For chemical analysis, individual elements were then scanned again, and the specific peaks were deconvoluted and described sequentially, one by one. The Ce3d5 spectrum of the [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex involved two multiplet peaks at 903.1 eV (B) and 885.12 eV (A), respectively, that ascribed to the spin–orbit splitting into 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 [29,55,56]. These are further deconvoluted into four different peaks (S1, S2, P1, and P2) (Fig. 2 (b)) [29,55,56]. The smaller peaks at 899.6 (P1) and 882.35 eV (S1) are allotted to the Ce 3d9 4f2, whereas the larger peaks at 904.7 (P2) and 885.2 eV (S2) are produced from the Ce3d94f1, which indicate that the Ce element existing as Ce3+ [55,56]. In the XPS spectrum of carbon, the C1s peak was connected with four individual peaks with the binding energies at 284.21, 284.59, 285.18, and 287.76 eV exhibits due to C–C, C=C, C-O, and C=O bonds respectively (Fig. 2 (c)) [29,55]. Similarly, the chemical nature of O1s was deconvoluted into three peaks having the binding energies 530.52, 531.41, and 532.29 eV, attributed to Ce-O, H2O, and C-O/ C=O bond in complex, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2 (d) [29,57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence study and optimization of the sensing application
ce:section.ce:para: In most cases, the aromatic conjugated organic ligands are investigated for the highest emission property in the fluorescent metal–organic framework. The aromatics, π-rich conjugated ligands are generally known as light absorbers [54,55]. Some metal–organic complexes have an intense metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT), which is characteristic in the visible region of spectra. Consequently, they can be good material for optical (like fluorescence), photoelectronic materials, biomedical imaging via magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and computed tomography (CT) [58,59]. Aside from individually organic linker-based photoluminescence have also been found several kinds of charge transfer processes, such as inter-ligand charge transfer (ILCT), metal–ligand charge transfer (MLCT), ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT), etc. have also been observed [60,61]. In addition, the metal–organic framework has also been studied in the previous few years as one of the most significant classes of materials in analytical applications due to its several unique properties, including diverse structural topologies, tunable surface area, diverse composition, and versatile functionality [62,63]. They are traditionally used for catalysis [64]. Especially the metals of lanthanide, which are the main participants in the case of metal node-based photoluminescence due to the Laporte forbidden f-f transitions [60]. Proceeding complexation with organic ligands, those organic compounds act as radiation absorbers and finally boost the emission intensity of the metal–organic framework. This effect is known as “photosensitization” or “antenna effect” [60,64,65]. Recently, fluorescent sensors have gained widespread acceptance and are extensively used for detecting toxic elements or metal ions (such as Hg, Pb, Cr, Cd, As, Bi, etc.) [66,67], as well as various drugs and biomolecules. This is because of their resistance to light scattering, non-destructive nature, high specificity and sensitivity, and ease of use.[29,68].

Therefore, we chose the π-rich aromatic conjugated ligand with cerium metal for the synthesis of fluorescent [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex. According to previous reports, π-rich aromatic ligands are responsible for the increased emission properties within the coordination complex via LMCT [60,61,69]. In the present work, on the addition of the analyte (INH drugs), the fluorescence intensity of the [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex is found to be quenched via electron transfer or energy transfer towards the analytes [29].

The fluorescent [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex was observed to exhibit an excitation spectrum within the excitation range from 200-420 nm while keeping an emission wavelength (λem) at 450 nm, as depicted in Fig. 3
(a). Regarding this coordination complex, two peaks are visible; the more intense peak can be seen at 302 nm, while the less intense peak can be seen at 252 nm. Further, the excitation-dependent PL character was also studied to optimize the most suitable emission intensity of the as-prepared [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex before utilizing it as a sensor probe. The fluorescent material (Fig. 3 (b)) is excited at a fixed wavelength interval from 250 − 310 nm. We observed that the fluorescence emission intensity of [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex first increases up to 302 nm excitation wavelength (λex), then decreases. Here, from this study, we confirmed that the best suitable excitation wavelength for this complex is λex = 302 nm because the highest emission intensity appears at this wavelength (Fig. 3 (b)). Likewise, the emission spectra of [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex were also recorded in the fluorescence emission range from 340-600 nm while keeping the fixed excitation wavelength at 302 nm, as shown in Fig. 3 (c). The emission spectrum exhibited at 457 nm due to 5d1 → 4f1 and 2D3/2 → 2F5/2 transitions [29,46,48,50].

Then, the pH condition of the fluorescent [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex is optimized by changing the pH range from 3 − 12, which is represented in Fig. 3 (d). This figure clearly shows that when the pH of the buffer solution rises (up to 3 to 6), the PL emission intensity of this complex first increases and then decreases. In this paper, the detection of INH was performed at physiological pH = 7.4 with acceptable PL activity because the pH value of the physiological biological system is 7.4. Subsequently, emission spectra of o-van, INH, [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3], and [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] in the presence of 82.8 μM INH (i.e. [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] + INH) were also recorded at λex = 302 nm and pH = 7.4, as shown in Fig. 3 (c). It is found that, in contrast to [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3], both materials, INH drug, and o-Van ligand, showed extremely low emission intensities under similar conditions. In comparison, the [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex exhibited a very strong fluorescence emission intensity at 457 nm due to the 5d1 → 4f1 and 2D3/2 → 2F5/2 transitions [29,46,48,50]. The emission intensity of [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] is quenched on the addition of INH (Fig. 5 (b)) as an effect of M → L charge transfer becoming delocalized towards analytes [29,70,71], as per the chemical reaction shown in Fig. 4 c.

As illustrated in Fig. 4 (a), the fluorescence emission spectra of the [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex were measured by adding the different concentrations of INH, ranging from 14.47 μM to 147.966 μM, in 2 ml solution of [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex at an excitation wavelength (λex = 302 nm). The data indicate that as the concentration of INH rises, the complex's PL intensity gradually decreases. Fig. 4 (b) presents the calibration plot of (F0–F)/F0 versus the concentration of INH, illustrating the photoluminescence (PL) quenching. Here, F0–F ​ represents the PL intensity without INH, and F is the PL intensity in the presence of INH. This plot shows a linear response over the INH concentration range of 14.47 μM to 147.966 μM, which is described by the linear regression equation (F0–F)/F0 = 0.0365 + 0.0052 [INH] with a correlation coefficient of 0.994. Additionally, the limit of detection (LOD) for the proposed sensor was found to be 1.63 μM, based on a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3.

To investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism in the presence of the analyte, we investigated the as-synthesized coordination complex and the complex mixed with INH using time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy, which is shown in Fig. 5
a. We used a green laser to excite the samples, and then we recorded the fluorescence intensity profiles over time to make sure that all samples were measured using the same parameters. The decay profiles were fitted to a single exponential decay (Fig. 5 a.) function,

, where τ corresponds to the excited state lifetime of the as-prepared coordination complex and

and a are fitting constants [29,37]; all the parameters are tabulated in Table 1 .

From the analysis in Table 1, it was observed that the average lifetime of the excited states for the [Ce(o-Van)3(H2O)3] complex decreases in the presence of INH. This reduction in lifetime suggests fluorescence emission quenching, likely due to the depopulation of the excited states and the resulting decrease in fluorescence lifetime.

Selectivity and specificity are critical characteristics of any sensor. A sequence of experimentations was conducted to evaluate the selectivity of the synthesized fluorescent sensor and assess its ability to detect INH selectively. Various commonly encountered compounds and ions were carefully selected as potential interfering agents in the sensing application. Throughout the experiments, INH concentration was kept at 82.79 μM, and the relative errorwascontrolled to within ± 5 %. The selectivity of the sensor for INH was tested in the presence of many interfering agents, including lysine (Lys), uric acid (UA), and ascorbic acid (AA) at concentrations equal to that of INH. Additionally, significantly higher concentrations (approximately ten times) of D-glucose (Glu), L-cystine (Cys), bovine serum albumin (BSA), glycine (Gly), urea, creatine (Cre), Fe2+, Ca2+, K+, Na+, Mg2+, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, CO3 2−, NO3 −, and Cl− were used in the measurements. Out of all the interferences, only UA and Gly slightly reduced the emission intensity of the [Ce (o-Van)3 (H2O)3] complex, while Cys and Lys caused a slight increase in fluorescence intensity. In contrast, the other interfering agents, such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Glu, BSA, Gly, urea, Cre, SO4 2−, PO4 3−, CO3 2−, NO3 −, and Cl−, did not significantly affect the sensing of INH, as shown in Fig. 6 . These results confirm that the [Ce (o-Van)3 (H2O)3] sensor exhibits excellent selectivity for INH, providing a reliable and specific response for this analyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of INH in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the reliability of the developed sensor [Ce (o-Van)3 (H2O)3] further, it was tested with commercially available isoniazid tablets. For sample preparation, a Solonex 300 mg tablet was weighed, ground into a fine powder with the help of a mortar and pestle to ensure homogeneity, and subjected to sonication for approximately 3 h to achieve complete solubility. After that, the solution was centrifuged for 25 min at 9000 rpm and used to spike samples for recovery analysis. The sensor's response was noted, as exposed in Table 2 . Additionally, the sensor's performance was assessed to detect INH in a healthy individual's urine sample. The urine samples were incubated at 80 °C for 35 min and then diluted tenfold, following the same protocol as in the previous application. Various concentrations of INH (as detailed in Table 2) were spiked into the urine sample, and the fluorescent spectra were recorded under optimized conditions in a phosphate buffer solution. The spectra confirmed a substantial decrease in fluorescence intensity corresponding to the increasing concentrations of INH. Further, the performance of the sensor was measured to detect INH in a blood sample of a healthy person. For the INH sensing, blood serum was diluted 10 times using phosphate buffer solution and spiking with different concentrations of the INH (10, 20, 30 μM), and the fluorescent spectra were recorded. The spectra confirmed a substantial decrease in fluorescence intensity with the increasing concentrations of INH (Table 2). The developed sensor provided a satisfactory response for detecting INH in pharmaceutical isoniazid tablets, urine, and blood samples. The investigation reported excellent recovery rates ranging from 100 % to 105 %, confirming the sensor's high performance. The stability of the sensor was estimated by recording its response at various time intervals, as shown in Fig. S2 of electronic supplementary material (ESI). The bar diagram indicates that the sensor maintained a stable response over the observed period, with no significant reduction in signal intensity. This consistency highlights the sensor’s reliability and robustness for long-term applications. The minimal variation in signal over time suggests that the sensor does not suffer from degradation or drift, issues commonly encountered in many sensing devices. The findings of the present study have been compared with previous reports, as summarized in Table 3 . This comparative analysis highlights the significance of our work in the field of INH sensing, demonstrating its advantages and improvements over earlier studies.

In this study, we successfully synthesized a fluorescent [Ce (o-Van)3 (H2O)3] complex probe and characterized it by using FT-IR, UV–visible spectroscopy, XPS, XRD, TGA and SEM. Our findings demonstrate that the analyte, isoniazid (INH), can effectively replace a coordinated water molecule within the complex, leading to a quenched fluorescence emission intensity. This quenching is ascribed to the delocalization of metal-to-ligand charge transfer (M → L) toward the analyte. The developed sensor displayed a robust linear response range from 14.47 μM to 147.966 μM, with a high linear correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.994. The sensor's lower LOD for INH was determined to be 1.63 μM. Furthermore, the fluorescent probe proved effective in detecting INH in commercially available isoniazid tablets, urine and blood samples of healthy human. These results highlight the potential of the [Ce (o-Van)3 (H2O)3] complex as a reliable, selective, and sensitive sensor for isoniazid detection in both pharmaceutical and biological samples.
